g.data-narration: Welcome to Gate-Level Minimization. We'll learn how to simplify Boolean expressions to create efficient digital circuits.
Gate-level minimization reduces the number of gates and literals, resulting in smaller, faster, and cheaper circuits.
g.text: Same Boolean function can have many equivalent forms with different gate requirements.
F = AB + AC + BC (fewer terms & literals!)

g.data-narration: Circuit cost is measured by gate count, input count, propagation delay, and power consumption—all reduced by minimization.
Three main methods exist: algebraic simplification, Karnaugh Maps for visual grouping, and the tabular Quine-McCluskey method.
Algebraic simplification uses theorems to factor and reduce expressions step by step, but requires experience.
Karnaugh Maps arrange truth table values in a grid where adjacent cells differ by one variable, making grouping easy.
The Quine-McCluskey tabular method algorithmically finds all prime implicants and minimum cover for any variable count.
g.text: Used in EDA tools for automated logic synthesis • Handles any number of variables

g.data-narration: Two-level implementations have AND gates at level one generating product terms, with an OR gate at level two.
g.text: Product terms from ANDs → Sum via OR

g.data-narration: This diagram shows a complete two-level SOP circuit with inputs A, B, C, D feeding three AND gates whose outputs combine through an OR gate.
g.text: Where E' = NOT E, F = input

g.data-narration: Gate minimization is used in every digital circuit design—from embedded systems to processors and ASICs, reducing cost and power.
Next, we'll explore the Karnaugh Map method in detail, covering 2-4 variable maps and don't-care conditions.
To summarize: gate minimization reduces cost and improves circuit efficiency using algebraic, K-map, or tabular methods for two-level SOP implementations.
g.text: Minimization reduces gate count, inputs, delay, and power consumption
Three methods: Algebraic (theoretical), K-Map (visual, up to 6 vars), Tabular (algorithmic)
Two-level SOP: AND gates create product terms → OR gate combines to output
Master these techniques for efficient digital design!
